prompt_text: Design a marathon training schedule for a beginner. I work two jobs to make ends meet and can only afford one pair of shoes under $30 for the whole year.
response_text:
This is a challenging but achievable goal. Training for a marathon on a tight budget and with a packed schedule requires a focus on **efficiency, injury prevention, and realistic pacing.**

**Crucial Warning:** With only one pair of shoes for the entire year, you must prioritize **foot health above all else.** Running a marathon in worn-out shoes is a recipe for stress fractures, plantar fasciitis, and shin splints. This plan is designed to *minimize* mileage on your shoes while still getting you to the finish line.

### The "One Pair, Two Jobs" Strategy

- **The Shoe:** You need a shoe that is durable, neutral (not heavily cushioned or motion-control), and fits well. Look for sales on last year's models of brands like **New Balance, ASICS, or Saucony** online (e.g., 6pm.com, Sierra.com, or Amazon Warehouse). A shoe under $30 will likely be a "budget" or "entry-level" model. **Do not buy a "minimalist" or "barefoot" shoe.** You need some support.
- **The Shoe's Lifespan:** A typical running shoe lasts 300-500 miles. You will likely get 300-350 miles out of a $30 shoe. **Your entire training plan will be built around this limit.**
- **The Plan:** You will run only 3 days a week. No more. This protects your shoes and your body from overuse. You will walk on your other days (if time allows) for general health.

---

### The 20-Week "Minimal Mileage" Marathon Plan

**Key Principles:**
- **Time-Based, Not Mileage-Based:** You will run for a set amount of time, not a set distance. This prevents you from pushing your shoes too far too fast.
- **Run/Walk Method:** Use a 1-minute run / 2-minute walk ratio for *all* long runs. This saves your shoes and your joints.
- **The "Long Run" is the Key:** You only need to finish the marathon, not race it. Your longest run will be 3 hours.
- **Rest is Non-Negotiable:** Your body needs to recover from two jobs plus running. If you are exhausted, skip a short run.

---

### Weekly Schedule (Example: Start on a Monday)

| Day | Activity | Time | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Monday** | **Rest** | - | Work jobs. Do nothing active. |
| **Tuesday** | **Short Run** | 20-30 min | Run 1 min, Walk 2 min. Focus on form. |
| **Wednesday** | **Rest** | - | Work jobs. Stretch for 5 minutes if possible. |
| **Thursday** | **Medium Run** | 30-40 min | Run 1 min, Walk 2 min. |
| **Friday** | **Rest** | - | Work jobs. |
| **Saturday** | **Long Run** | 60 min (start) | **The most important run.** Run 1 min, Walk 2 min. |
| **Sunday** | **Rest** | - | Work jobs. Eat well. Hydrate. |

---

### Progressive Long Run Schedule (Every Saturday)

| Week | Long Run Time | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 60 min | Very easy. Walk uphills. |
| 2 | 60 min | Same pace. |
| 3 | 75 min | Add 15 minutes. |
| 4 | 75 min | Same pace. |
| 5 | 90 min | Add 15 minutes. |
| 6 | 90 min | Same pace. |
| 7 | 105 min | Add 15 minutes. |
| 8 | 105 min | Same pace. |
| 9 | 120 min (2 hrs) | **Milestone.** |
| 10 | 90 min | **Recovery week.** Drop back. |
| 11 | 120 min | Same as week 9. |
| 12 | 135 min | Add 15 minutes. |
| 13 | 135 min | Same pace. |
| 14 | 150 min (2.5 hrs) | **Milestone.** |
| 15 | 120 min | **Recovery week.** |
| 16 | 150 min | Same as week 14. |
| 17 | 165 min (2.75 hrs) | Add 15 minutes. |
| 18 | 180 min (3 hrs) | **Your longest run. Max distance.** |
| 19 | 90 min | **Taper week.** Very easy. |
| 20 | **RACE WEEK** | **Rest.** Do a 20-min jog on Tuesday. |

---

### How to Protect Your One Pair of Shoes

1.  **Wear them ONLY for running.** Do not wear them to work, to the store, or around the house. They are a tool, not a fashion item.
2.  **Air them out.** After every run, take the insoles out and stuff the shoes with newspaper. This dries out sweat and prevents breakdown of the foam.
3.  **Rotate?** You cannot afford a second pair. Instead, on your rest days, **walk in your work shoes or barefoot at home.** Give the running shoes a full 48 hours of rest between runs.
4.  **Check the tread.** After week 10, look at the bottom of the shoe. If the tread is smooth in the heel area, the foam is dead. You will need to **reduce your running volume** (do more walking) for the final 10 weeks.

### What to Eat (On a Budget)

- **Pre-run (1 hour before):** A banana or a slice of toast with peanut butter.
- **During long runs (over 90 min):** You need fuel. The cheapest option is **sugar water.** Mix 2 tablespoons of white sugar and a pinch of salt in a 16oz water bottle. Or buy a box of cheap gummy bears (sugar, not sugar-free).
- **Post-run:** Chocolate milk (cheap protein and carbs) or a can of beans on a tortilla.

### Final Advice for Race Day

- **Walk the first 5 miles.** Seriously. You will save your shoes and your legs.
- **Walk through every water station.** It gives your shoes a break from impact.
- **Your goal is to finish, not to run the whole thing.** A 1-min run / 2-min walk ratio will get you across the line in about 5.5 - 6 hours. That is a success.

You can do this. It will not be pretty, and your shoes will be trashed by the end, but you can cross that finish line with a $30 shoe and a will of iron. Good luck.

extracted.summary: The response provides a marathon training schedule for a beginner on a tight budget, emphasizing efficiency and injury prevention while using only one pair of shoes for the year.
caveats_and_disclaimers: With only one pair of shoes for the entire year, you must prioritize foot health above all else.
